title: Managing Your Career as a Product
subtitle: Personal brand, networking, industry visibility. How to sell your skills at a premium
description: This checklist shifts the paradigm of career thinking: you are not an 'employee looking for a job', but a 'product solving specific market problems'. We'll cover how to package your skills into a value proposition, build a personal brand that creates an inbound flow of opportunities, and master the art of networking without being pushy. Your salary is not a judgment of you as a person, but the price the market is willing to pay for solving a specific problem. Learn to manage this price.

items.title: Career as a Product: Fundamental Mindset Shift
items.source: Cal Newport — So Good They Can't Ignore You
items.text: Traditional approach: 'I have a degree and experience, I'm looking for a job.' Entrepreneurial approach: 'I am a product solving specific problems for a specific audience for specific money.' The difference is colossal. In the first case, you are a supplicant, competing with hundreds of similar resumes. In the second, an expert people seek out. Cal Newport calls this 'career capital' — the combination of rare and valuable skills you can offer the market. The question is not 'What job can I get?', but 'What well-paying problem can I solve better than others?' This requires an inventory of your skills, an honest assessment of their market value, and a strategy for building scarce competencies.
items.tip: Conduct a skills audit. Divide them into three categories: 1) Commodity (everyone has them, low price), 2) Specialized (many in your niche have them, medium price), 3) Rare & Valuable (few have them, high price). Focus on developing the third category.

items.title: Value Proposition: The Formula 'I Help X Achieve Y Through Z'
items.text: A resume listing responsibilities ('responsible for marketing', 'managed projects') doesn't sell. It describes actions, not results. Your value proposition should answer three questions: Who is your target audience (X)? What measurable result do you help them achieve (Y)? What unique method do you use (Z)? For example: 'I help B2B SaaS companies at the growth stage (X) increase trial-to-paid conversion by 30% within 3 months (Y) by implementing personalized onboarding and behavioral data analysis (Z).' This formulation immediately positions you as a solution, not just another candidate. Employers and clients don't buy your time; they buy specific outcomes.
items.tip: Formulate your value proposition using the formula 'I help [whom] achieve [what result] through [what method].' Check: Is the result measurable? Does it sound attractive to decision-makers with money?

items.title: Personal Brand: Not Self-Promotion, But an Asset
items.text: Personal brand is not about selfies and inspirational quotes. It's a managed reputation as an expert in a specific niche. When a recruiter or potential client Googles your name, they should see confirmation of your expertise: articles, talks, comments in professional media, recommendations from colleagues. A personal brand works for you 24/7, creating an inbound flow of opportunities. Key channels for building: LinkedIn (professional content), industry conferences (speaking), professional communities (active participation), creating 'content assets' (articles, podcasts, videos) that continue to provide value for years. Important: a personal brand is built around a single key theme in which you want to be known as an expert.
items.tip: Choose one narrow niche where you want to be 'the face'. For example, not just 'marketer', but 'marketer for EdTech startups specializing in organic growth'. All your public activity should reinforce this focus.

items.title: LinkedIn as Your Product Showcase
items.source: Richard van der Blom — LinkedIn Algorithm Report
items.text: LinkedIn is not a digital resume, but your professional showcase, open 24/7 to the global market. Key packaging elements: 1) Headline — not a job title, but a value proposition (e.g., not 'Product Manager at X', but 'Helping B2B Products Grow from 0 to 100k Users'). 2) 'About' section — not a biography, but an address to your target audience describing their problems and your solutions. 3) Featured section — best case studies, articles, videos. 4) Activity — regular content showing your thinking. The LinkedIn algorithm rewards those who create discussion, not just broadcast. Comments on others' posts often get more reach than your own publications.
items.tip: Update your LinkedIn headline: remove your job title, insert the formula 'Helping [whom] achieve [result]'. Add 3-5 keywords recruiters should find you by.

items.title: Networking Without Pushiness: The Principle of 'Give, Not Take'
items.text: Most people hate 'networking' because they imagine it as collecting business cards and cold emails with requests. Real networking is building mutually valuable relationships long before you need something. Adam Grant's research showed: in the long run, 'givers' win — those who generously share knowledge, connections, and help without expecting immediate return. Practical strategy: 1) Regularly share useful content and insights in your niche. 2) Introduce people to each other if you see mutual benefit. 3) Offer specific help, not abstract 'let me know if you need anything'. 4) Maintain 'weak ties' — periodically remind people you don't work with daily of your existence.
items.tip: Each week, do one 'networking action' without asking for yourself: write a recommendation for a former colleague, share an article with a helpful comment, introduce two people in your network who might benefit from knowing each other.

items.title: Industry Visibility: The 'Three Pillars' Strategy
items.text: Being a great specialist is not enough if no one knows about you. Visibility is not bragging; it's documenting your work process and demonstrating expertise. The 'Three Pillars' visibility strategy: 1) Written content — articles on professional platforms, LinkedIn posts, blogging about solving professional problems. 2) Speaking — presentations at conferences (from small meetups to large forums), webinars, podcasts. 3) Community participation — active help on professional forums, Slack communities, answering questions in Q&A. Start small: a comment in a professional chat, a short post about a solved problem, offering to present a case study at an internal company meetup.
items.tip: Keep a 'work diary' publicly. Once a week, write a post about what professional problem you solved, what difficulties you faced, and what lesson you learned. People value process, not just results.

items.title: Salary Negotiation: Selling Value, Not Asking for a Raise
items.text: Compensation negotiation is not a conflict or a request, but a business discussion about a fair price for the value you create. Key mistakes: 1) Starting with personal circumstances ('I have a mortgage'). Employers pay for results, not your needs. 2) Not preparing market data. Salary studies (Glassdoor, Levels.fyi, industry reports) are your objective anchor. 3) Discussing only base salary. Total compensation package includes: bonuses, options/equity, sign-on bonus, health insurance, training, flexible hours, extra vacation. 4) Accepting the first offer without trying to negotiate. Research shows negotiators earn 7-10% more on average at start, which over a career amounts to hundreds of thousands of dollars.
items.tip: When preparing for negotiations, prepare a 'value dossier': a list of your specific achievements over the past year with numbers (increased X by Y%, saved Z rubles, launched a project worth N). Speak the language of business results.

items.title: Career Growth Through 'Intrapreneurship'
items.text: An intrapreneur is an employee who acts like an entrepreneur inside a company: seeks new opportunities, initiates projects, takes responsibility for results, not just follows orders. Such employees grow faster and earn more because they create unique value that is hard to replace. How to become an intrapreneur: 1) Look for business 'pains' that no one is solving. 2) Propose not a problem, but a solution with a rough calculation of effect. 3) Take on projects at the intersection of your current role and your next desired position. 4) Document results and make them visible to management.
items.tip: Find one 'pain' in your company that lies outside your direct responsibilities. Develop a mini-proposal for solving it and present it to your manager in the format: 'I noticed problem X, here's how it can be solved with minimal cost, I'm ready to take this on.' This demonstrates leadership.

items.title: The Hidden Job Market and How to Access It
items.source: Lou Adler — Hire With Your Head
items.text: Estimates suggest 50% to 80% of good positions are never publicly posted. This is the 'hidden job market', filled through referrals, networking, and direct headhunting. To access this market, you need to be visible and memorable in your professional niche. Practical steps: 1) Become 'the person who knows about X' in your professional community. 2) Maintain relationships with recruiters in your industry, even when not job searching. 3) Periodically network with colleagues from other companies at professional events. 4) Be open to 'informational interviews' — when someone wants to learn about your company or role, agree. Often these conversations turn into offers.
items.tip: Make a list of 10-15 companies you'd like to work for. Find employees of those companies on LinkedIn (especially those at a level adjacent to yours). Write to them not with a job request, but with an industry question: 'I follow your company, impressed by project X. Could you share what challenges your department is currently facing?'

items.title: Strategic Career Planning for 3-5 Years
items.source: Reid Hoffman — The Start-Up of You
items.text: Career in the modern world is not a ladder you climb in one company, but a series of 'tours of duty', each lasting 2-4 years and giving you new experience, skills, and connections. Reid Hoffman, founder of LinkedIn, suggests treating your career as a startup under uncertainty: have a long-term vision, but be flexible in tactics. A career plan should not be rigid 'in 5 years I'll be a director', but should contain answers to: What competencies do I want to acquire in the next 3 years? In which industries and companies are these competencies valued? What projects and roles will give me such experience? What network of contacts do I need for this?
items.tip: Every six months, conduct a 'career retro': what new things did I learn in the last 6 months? What skills did I acquire? How much closer am I to my vision? Do I need to adjust course? This prevents drifting with the current.

quiz.q: What is 'career capital' according to Cal Newport?
a: The set of rare and valuable skills you can offer the market
Number of years of experience on a resume
